::: title
Le Grand Jeu
:::

::: edition
Game Master\'s Manual & Cheatsheet · English Edition
:::
:::

::: cover-tag
A guide for the person who runs the economy, reads the room, spins the
wheel, and says **\"Yes, and how?\"** to whatever the table invents
next.
:::

::: cover-byline
[A GAME BY]{.by} Federico Bonelli & Raffaella Rovida
:::

legrandjeu.net · CC BY-NC-SA 4.0

::: page
::: run-header
[What\'s different about LGJ]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# Before you run your first table

If you\'ve run a D&D session, a Blades in the Dark heist, or any
improv-heavy TTRPG, you already have most of the skills. But LGJ will
ask you to suppress a few reflexes and develop others. Read this before
your first game.

::: intro-grid
::: intro-card
::: intro-card-label
Reflex to suppress
:::

::: intro-card-title
The adversary posture
:::

::: intro-card-body
You are not the players\' opponent. There is no BBEG. The world itself
--- its physics, scarcity, and consequences --- is the opposition. Your
job is to model it honestly, not to defeat the table.
:::
:::

::: intro-card
::: intro-card-label
Reflex to suppress
:::

::: intro-card-title
Plot-driven pacing
:::

::: intro-card-body
There is no story you are steering toward. The story is emergent; the
round structure is the metronome. If nothing dramatic happens for two
rounds, that\'s fine --- the drama will come from the wheel, the
economy, or an assembly.
:::
:::

::: intro-card
::: intro-card-label
Reflex to suppress
:::

::: intro-card-title
Rule-of-cool resolution
:::

::: intro-card-body
\"That sounds awesome, roll for it\" works in TTRPGs. In LGJ, awesome
ideas still need a cost, a yield, and a grey footprint. Beauty is not an
economic argument --- but it can earn social value.
:::
:::
:::

## Reflexes to develop instead

### Thinking in economics, not fiction

Every player proposal needs three numbers before it can be played: a
**construction cost** (one-off), an **operation cost** (per round), and
a **yield** (usually a dice expression). You will improvise these the
way a D&D GM improvises DCs --- fast, calibrated, with a table for
reference.

### Keeping the economy visible

Tokens on the table are not bookkeeping --- they are the game. Players
read the two collective pots, their own stacks, and each other\'s at a
glance. Never resolve a transaction mentally; always move physical
tokens. When in doubt, slow down and show the math.

### Facilitating, not arbitrating

When a player invents something you\'ve never seen --- a carbon-credit
market, a mutual-aid cooperative, a solar farm --- don\'t rule from the
chair. Ask the table: *\"Does this sound like it should cost more than a
shop or less? Should it produce grey?\"* The group\'s collective
knowledge is your calibration instrument.

::: callout-dark
::: callout-label
The golden rule
:::

**\"Yes, and how?\"**  --- borrowed from improv theatre. Whatever a
player proposes, your default is acceptance. The only question is *how*
it enters the game: at what cost, with what yield, with what grey
footprint, on or off the grid. Saying \"no\" is a last resort and almost
always a sign you haven\'t yet asked the right \"how\".
:::

## The GM\'s three focus dials

During play, keep a part of your attention on three meters. Each has an
opposite pole. Your job is to notice when one is drifting to an extreme
and nudge back.

::: dial-row
::: dial
::: dial-label
Stress
:::

::: dial-poles
Challenged ↔ Empowered
:::

::: dial-body
Players should feel the economy bite, but never feel the game is rigged.
If someone is visibly frustrated for two rounds, give them a cheap win
or a fortune card.
:::
:::

::: dial
::: dial-label
Artist\'s block
:::

::: dial-poles
Open ↔ Structured
:::

::: dial-body
Too much freedom and players freeze. Too much structure and they stop
inventing. If ideas dry up, offer a concrete example. If ideas sprawl,
ask for a cost.
:::
:::

::: dial
::: dial-label
Engagement
:::

::: dial-poles
Participation ↔ Tempo
:::

::: dial-body
Ensure every player has spoken each round. If the loudest voice has
dominated three turns, give the spotlight explicitly. Keep rounds under
\~20 minutes.
:::
:::
:::

::: pagenum
2 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[Before the game]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# Session zero

Half the quality of a game is decided before any dice roll. Use 10--15
minutes at the top of the session to make these choices deliberately
rather than by default.

## Choose the scenario

A **scenario** is a situation that frames the session: the place, its
resources, its stresses, its question. The SPES scenario asks \"can we
build a society inside planetary boundaries?\". A textile-waste scenario
asks \"what do we do with the bales that keep arriving?\". Choose one
from the scenario pack, or write your own on a scenario card.

If this is the table\'s first game, prefer a **simple, local scenario**
(a single bioregion, one river, no exotic technology). Complexity
compounds fast.

## Brief the players

Describe the world in two minutes: geography, population, climate,
access to grid. Let players ask questions --- but avoid over-specifying.
Gaps are invitations for their invention.

Introduce the **two currencies** physically: hold up a white, hold up a
grey, say what each means for this scenario (money/resources/knowledge
vs pollution/depletion/waste). In an industrial scenario grey is mostly
CO₂; in a rural one it might be soil erosion or water loss. Name it.

## Set the governance rule

Flip a coin: *heads* = majority, *tails* = consensus. This determines
how assemblies resolve. Consensus produces richer discussion but can
stall; majority is faster but can exclude. Honour the coin --- don\'t
\"re-flip until the one you wanted.\"

## Tune the wheel

Default proportions are 50% LGJ (collective) / 20% Fortune (personal) /
15% Invent / 15% Destroy. The scenario may override this. A crisis
scenario can go 70% LGJ. A \"building peacetime\" scenario might drop
Destroy to 5%.

## Hold the victory rule until asked

Don\'t announce how to win. Wait for the first player to ask. Who asks
first --- and what they assume victory means --- tells you a lot about
what this table wants from the game. Then reveal the rule, or ask the
table to choose one of: individual score, collective survival,
lowest-waste table.

## Roll starting resources with the table watching

1.  Each player rolls [1d6]{.dice} for starting territories and places
    them on or off the grid, narrating what the land is used for.
2.  Each player rolls [2d6]{.dice} whites and [1d6]{.dice} greys --- the
    greys are starting baggage (legacy pollution, debts, accumulated
    impact), not a penalty.
3.  Each player names their starting activity. You write it on an
    element piece with cost and yield --- see page 5 for calibration.

::: callout
::: callout-label
Facilitator note
:::

If the table is diverse (language, experience, power), seed the starting
positions yourself rather than by random roll --- give the quieter
players slightly more land, or the off-grid option they asked for. This
is one of the places where the GM\'s discretion legitimately overrides
the dice.
:::

## Explain the tax, then start

On-grid players pay 1 white per round into the local pot. This is
visible, unavoidable, and frames the first question any new player asks:
*\"should I go off-grid?\"* --- which is the exact conversation the game
wants to provoke.

::: pagenum
3 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[Running the economy · part 1]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# Costing, yielding, calibrating

The core mechanical job. Every activity on the board is an **element**,
and every element has four values. Your skill as GM is improvising those
values fast, fairly, and in proportion to what\'s already on the table.

## Element anatomy

::: {style="display:flex;gap:6mm;align-items:flex-start;margin:3mm 0 4mm"}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

<div>

**Construction cost** --- whites paid once, when the element is first
built. Inherited starting activities have cost zero.

**Operation cost** --- whites and/or greys paid every round the element
is active. Can be zero.

**Yield** --- a dice expression. Player rolls each round they operate
the element, receives that many whites. Some elements also produce greys
as a by-product --- note a grey coin next to the yield.

</div>
:::

## Calibration in four questions

When a player proposes a new activity, ask yourself (or the table):

1.  **How hard is this to build?** Hand-tool craft = 0--2 whites. Small
    shop = 2--4. Factory or hospital = 6--12. Major infrastructure =
    15+. This is the construction cost.
2.  **What does it consume every round it runs?** A farm eats labour and
    water. A hospital burns energy and supplies. A festival consumes
    time but almost nothing material. This is the operation cost.
3.  **What does it produce, and how reliably?** Reliable, modest =
    [d4]{.dice}. Normal, variable = [d6]{.dice} or [2d6]{.dice}. Large
    but risky = [d10]{.dice} or [d20]{.dice}. This is the yield dice.
4.  **Where does the grey come from?** Burning anything, extracting
    anything, mass-producing anything. If the activity is net clean ---
    gardening, teaching, performing --- no grey. If ambiguous, mark
    [1d6]{.dice}: on a 1 it pollutes this round.

::: callout
::: callout-label
When in doubt, ask the table
:::

\"Does a community solar farm feel closer to a shop or a hospital?\"  
\"Would you say a barter market produces grey?\"   The table\'s
consensus is not the truth, but it\'s a truth the game can run on. Write
it down in the master\'s diary for next time.
:::

## The first round exception

In round one, each player\'s *starting* activity is inherited and pays
no construction cost. They do, however, pay operation cost from round
one onward. You still set a yield --- typically [d6]{.dice} for a modest
subsistence activity.

## Handling \"Invent\"

When a player picks the Invent action, they\'re not deploying a ready
activity --- they\'re researching something that doesn\'t yet exist on
the board. Process:

1.  Player describes the invention in 30 seconds.
2.  Table discusses for 60--90 seconds: is this plausible? What might it
    cost to research? What could it yield if it worked?
3.  You set a **probability of success** (a threshold on [1d20]{.dice}
    --- typically 10--15 for plausible ideas, higher for breakthroughs)
    and a **research cost** (3--6 whites plus 1--2 greys for lab waste
    is a fair baseline).
4.  Player pays. Rolls. On success, the invention becomes a new element
    with its own cost/yield/grey, which you write up fresh. On failure,
    the cost is lost but the knowledge is banked --- next attempt gets a
    +2 bonus.

::: pagenum
4 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[Running the economy · part 2]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# Example activities

A calibration library. Use these as anchor points when players propose
something new. Each entry shows construction cost, operation per round,
yield, and what the activity *resembles* --- so a \"bike courier\" can
be statted as \"like transports, minus the vehicle overhead, grey drops
to ambiguous\".

::: cluster-head
Procurement & primary production
:::

  Activity         Build                Per round                               Yield   Resembles / differs in
  ---------------- -------------------- --------------------------------------- ------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Small farm       1[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d6      Gardener with more scale. Orchard resembles, but slower: yield [d6]{.dice} only every 2 rounds.
  Gardener         0                    0                                       2       Subsistence baseline. Beekeeper similar, add [d4]{.dice} honey yield.
  Fishing boat     3[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d8      Like small farm but water-based. Add grey on [d10]{.dice}=1 for overfishing.
  Quarry / mine    6[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w} 1[]{.coin .coin-g}   2d6     High yield, reliable grey. Timber logging resembles, with [d8]{.dice} yield.
  Well / aquifer   2[]{.coin .coin-w}   0                                       d4      Infrastructural --- serves multiple neighbours. Solar panel resembles for electricity.

::: cluster-head
Production & craft
:::

  Activity             Build                Per round                               Yield   Resembles / differs in
  -------------------- -------------------- --------------------------------------- ------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Small shop           2[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d6      Bakery, newsagent, greengrocer all fit here.
  Packaging workshop   2[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w} 2[]{.coin .coin-g}   2d6     High grey (plastics, print). Printing press resembles with lower grey.
  Food processing      3[]{.coin .coin-w}   3[]{.coin .coin-w} 1[]{.coin .coin-g}   3d6     Jam factory, cannery, dairy. Resembles brewery; replace grey with water stress.
  Mechanic workshop    2[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d8      Computer / phone repair resembles. Pollution moves from oil & metal to e-waste; location moves from street to indoor.
  Artisan craft        1[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d6      Ceramist, weaver, luthier. Low grey, slow scaling.
  Construction crew    3[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w} 1[]{.coin .coin-g}   d8      Builds other people\'s elements at 50% cost. Demolition crew resembles, yields salvage [d4]{.dice}.

::: cluster-head
Services & knowledge (2.0)
:::

  Activity          Build                Per round             Yield          Resembles / differs in
  ----------------- -------------------- --------------------- -------------- --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Hospital          6[]{.coin .coin-w}   3[]{.coin .coin-w}    5 + d20        Public good. On [d20]{.dice} ≤ 3 a crisis consumes an extra 3[]{.coin .coin-w} this round. Private clinic resembles but pockets the yield.
  School            4[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w}    2d6 literacy   Yield is social value, not whites. University resembles with a +3 research bonus to any Invent roll in the bioregion.
  Festival          0                    d6[]{.coin .coin-w}   d6 ±3          Burns grey ([d6]{.dice} from the atmosphere pot). Enjoy action uses this statline by default.
  Worker            0                    0                     d2             A person\'s labour. Accountant resembles at [d4]{.dice}; business consultant at [d8]{.dice}. Each skill tier is a Learn action.
  Research centre   5[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w}    d4             Yield is modest whites but grants +30% success rate to any Invent by a connected player.
  Open-source lab   3[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}    d4             Like research centre but +20% to everyone, and all inventions are shared --- no proprietary advantage.
  Repair café       1[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}    d4             Reduces other players\' grey by [d4]{.dice} per round. Social value +1 each round it runs.

::: cluster-head
Infrastructure & logistics
:::

  Activity             Build                Per round                               Yield                                   Resembles / differs in
  -------------------- -------------------- --------------------------------------- --------------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Transports           3[]{.coin .coin-w}   2[]{.coin .coin-w} 1[]{.coin .coin-g}   d8                                      Moves resources between non-adjacent territories. On [d10]{.dice}=1 a breakdown: no yield, +1 grey.
  Bike courier         1[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}                      d4                                      Transports minus the vehicle overhead. Range limited to adjacent territories. No grey.
  Fossil power plant   8[]{.coin .coin-w}   0                                       3[]{.coin .coin-w} 5[]{.coin .coin-g}   Feeds the grid. Yields to the local pot, not a single player. Covers 4 triangles. Always-on grey tax.
  RES microgrid        6[]{.coin .coin-w}   0                                       2[]{.coin .coin-w} × var                Renewable. Each round spin a variability wheel (0%/50%/100%) for actual yield. No grey.

::: cluster-head
Edge cases
:::

  Activity              Build                Per round            Yield   Resembles / differs in
  --------------------- -------------------- -------------------- ------- ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Smuggling             1[]{.coin .coin-w}   1[]{.coin .coin-w}   d12     High yield. 40% to start; 10% per round of being caught --- if caught, the element is destroyed and the player pays a fine of its yield value.
  Informal settlement   0                    0                    0       Off-grid shelter. No yield, no tax, no eviction unless an assembly votes it. Social value −1 per round it lacks infrastructure.

::: pagenum
5 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[Facilitation craft]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# Reading the room

The numbers are a scaffold. The game happens in what players say between
rolls --- and your job is to make that conversation as productive as
possible.

## Prompting questions

When a player is stuck, don\'t suggest an activity. Ask a question that
opens the space. A small library of prompts, grouped by what\'s stuck:

### When someone can\'t think of what to build

-   What did your character do before the game started?
-   What does this bioregion need that no one is providing?
-   Who in your real life does something that could go on this board?
-   What would you build if you had twice the whites?

### When the table is too individualistic

-   Whose activity depends on yours --- directly, even if neither of you
    noticed?
-   What does this bioregion have that none of you own individually?
-   Is there anything it would be cheaper to build together?

### When someone is about to do something destructive

-   Who else does this affect?
-   What would the land say if it could?
-   Is this a one-round decision or a permanent one?
-   What would you need to feel ok *not* doing this?

## Spotlight management

Track who has spoken this round. If one player has dominated three
actions, on the next round call on the quietest player first --- not by
embarrassing, but by routing: *\"You\'re closest to the river, do you
see the spill from your land?\"* Giving them a concrete detail to
respond to is easier than asking for an open contribution.

If two players start negotiating privately during another player\'s
turn, don\'t stop them --- but loop back: *\"Hold that thought; we\'ll
come to it after this turn.\"* Assemblies are where private negotiations
become public.

## Pacing

Each round should take 15--20 minutes in a 4-player game. If a round
goes over 30, something is stuck --- usually a proposal that can\'t be
costed. Break the loop: write any number that feels fair, say \"we\'ll
revisit this in debrief\", move on.

The wheel at end of round is a natural energy reset. Make it physical:
get up, walk over, spin, return. The small interruption protects against
the table getting lost in one player\'s spiral.

## When to interrupt, when to let it play

Let bad ideas play. If a player wants to build a plastic-packaging
megafactory next to the river, don\'t argue them out of it --- stat it
honestly, let the grey hit the table, and watch how the other players
respond. The game teaches faster than you can.

Interrupt only when:

-   A player is about to violate a rule they didn\'t know existed
    (surprise \"gotcha\" is cruel).
-   The activity needs a crucial calibration check with the table
    (don\'t decide alone on a player\'s career-ending invention).
-   Someone at the table has gone visibly quiet in a way that suggests
    discomfort, not disengagement.

::: callout
::: callout-label
A tell worth learning
:::

When a player starts explaining their idea in *legal* language --- \"am
I *allowed* to?\", \"does the rule say I can?\" --- the game has drifted
from improv to arbitration. Redirect: *\"Tell me what you want to do,
not whether you can.\"* The rules are for resolving outcomes, not for
requesting permission.
:::

::: pagenum
6 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[Events, crisis & after]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

# The wheel, the ceiling, the debrief

## Reading events aloud

When the wheel lands on an LGJ or Fortune sector, don\'t read the card
flatly. Read the title, pause, ask one player to describe what that
would look like on this bioregion, *then* apply the mechanical effect.
The card is a prompt, not a verdict.

For Invent and Destroy sectors, no card is drawn. You narrate an event
that feels native to this scenario --- a failed harvest, a rumour of a
new technology, a fire in the next valley. Keep it short (30 seconds),
let the table decide who is affected and how.

## Modelling fire, pollution, crisis

Some events cascade. A pattern that works:

-   **Fire** --- spin the wheel for wind direction. Downwind triangles
    roll [d6]{.dice}: on 1--2 they ignite next round. An ignited
    triangle loses whatever is built on it. Put it out by surrounding it
    with whites (1 white per adjacent triangle).
-   **Pollution plume** --- when grey in a region exceeds 10, adjacent
    territories lose [d4]{.dice} yield until it clears. Clears only by
    collective action.
-   **Epidemic** --- each player rolls [d10]{.dice}; on 1--3 loses
    [d4]{.dice} whites (productivity, healthcare costs). A hospital on
    the grid halves the damage.

## When grey approaches 50

Don\'t hide the ecological ceiling from players. Announce it at 30, 40,
45. At 48, the table should be visibly nervous. If grey hits 50, end the
game in narrative: describe the collapse, then stop.

A good session often *does* hit the ceiling. Don\'t treat this as a
failure to prevent --- it\'s the game doing its job.

## When a player is about to die

If someone\'s about to run out of whites, let them know one round ahead:
*\"If you don\'t earn 2 whites next round, your character is out.\"*
This is not saving them --- it\'s giving them the chance to ask for
help, call an assembly, or go off-grid. Elimination should never be a
surprise.

If they do die: 5 grey per remaining round into the atmosphere pot.
Narrate what they lose and what the bioregion loses with them. Invite
them to stay as narrator / observer for the rest of the session.

## After the game: 15 minutes of debrief

Every session gets at least a short debrief. Ask, in order:

1.  What did you build that you were proud of?
2.  What did the group get right? What did it miss?
3.  Did the wheel events feel real, contrived, or arbitrary?
4.  If we played this scenario again tomorrow, what would we do
    differently?
5.  What did this session tell you about the actual question the
    scenario asked?

Write the answers in the master\'s diary --- both for next session, and
because each table\'s answers reveal something the rules can\'t capture.

::: callout
::: callout-label
For workshops and research
:::

If this session is part of a research or documentation context (see the
SPES protocol), record the debrief with consent, photograph the final
board state, note every custom rule invented at the table. These are the
artefacts that make workshops reusable.
:::

::: pagenum
7 / 8
:::

::: footer
legrandjeu.net
:::
:::

::: page
::: run-header
[GM Cheatsheet · keep in front of you during play]{.sec}
![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,){.logo-rhombus-mini}
:::

::: mantra
**The Golden Rule** \"Yes, and how?\"
:::

::: cheat-grid
::: cheat-cell
## Round flow

1.  **Pay tax** --- on-grid players: 1[]{.coin .coin-w} to local pot.
2.  **Each player acts** --- one of: Develop, Invent, Assembly, Learn,
    Enjoy.
3.  **Spin the wheel** --- LGJ / Fortune / Invent / Destroy.
4.  **Resolve event**, move tokens visibly.
5.  **Check ceilings**: grey ≥ 50 ends the game.

## Action quick-ref

  Action     Roll    Notes
  ---------- ------- ----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Develop    1d10    GM sets cost & yield for the proposed venture.
  Invent     1d20    Pay 3--6[]{.coin .coin-w} + 1--2[]{.coin .coin-g}. Success ≥ 10--15.
  Assembly   vote    Majority or consensus per coin flip at setup.
  Learn      pay     GM sets cost of the skill/knowledge.
  Enjoy      d6 ±3   Burns grey. Social value +1.

## Calibration anchors

  Scale              Build                    Yield
  ------------------ ------------------------ -----------
  Hand craft         0--2[]{.coin .coin-w}    d4
  Small shop         2--4[]{.coin .coin-w}    d6
  Factory / clinic   6--12[]{.coin .coin-w}   2d6 / d10
  Infrastructure     15+[]{.coin .coin-w}     d12 / d20

## The three dials

-   **Stress** --- challenged ↔ empowered. Intervene if frustrated 2
    rounds.
-   **Artist\'s block** --- open ↔ structured. Offer example, or demand
    a cost.
-   **Engagement** --- participation ↔ tempo. Every player speaks per
    round.
:::

::: cheat-cell
## Setup checklist

-   Scenario chosen & briefed (2 min)
-   Currencies introduced physically
-   Coin flipped: majority or consensus?
-   Wheel tuned to scenario
-   Each player: 1d6 territory, 2d6[]{.coin .coin-w}, 1d6[]{.coin
    .coin-g}
-   Starting activity named & statted
-   Two pots (local + atmosphere) placed visibly
-   Victory rule held until first player asks

## Ceilings & endings

-   **Grey ≥ 50** → ecological collapse. Game ends.
-   **Player out of whites** → eliminated. +5[]{.coin .coin-g} per
    remaining round.
-   **Agreed rounds reached** (usually 5--8).
-   **Time up** (1.5--2 hours).

## Wheel sectors (default)

-   **LGJ 50%** --- collective card, affects all.
-   **Fortune 20%** --- personal card, one player.
-   **Invent 15%** --- GM narrates a new possibility.
-   **Destroy 15%** --- GM narrates a loss or setback.

## Victory options

-   **A · Individual** --- highest ([]{.coin .coin-w} − []{.coin
    .coin-g}) wins.
-   **B · Collective** --- survive ceiling, no one eliminated.
-   **C · Tournament** --- table with least total grey wins.

## Prompts when stuck

-   What does this bioregion need that no one is providing?
-   Whose activity depends on yours?
-   Who else does this affect?
-   What would the land say if it could?
-   Cost more or less than a shop? Than a hospital?

## Do & don\'t

-   **Do** move tokens physically, always.
-   **Do** ask the table when you\'re unsure.
-   **Do** announce the ceiling as it approaches.
-   **Don\'t** argue players out of bad ideas --- stat them honestly.
-   **Don\'t** surprise-eliminate a player.
-   **Don\'t** reveal victory until asked.
:::
:::

::: credit
